food_name: Yakitori — Mixed Grilled Chicken Skewers with Tare Sauce
food_subgroup: Grilled chicken skewers — mixed composition (thigh, skin, offal) with tare glaze

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific yakitori term; 'chicken kebab' (FOODON_03305931) is the closest defensible parent for skewered grilled chicken.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A012Z (Grilled poultry) is the closest defensible parent; no yakitori-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for yakitori or Japanese grilled chicken skewer identified in published SNOMED CT releases.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard order of yakitori at a Japanese izakaya is 5 skewers, yielding approximately 150g of edible grilled chicken (mixed thigh, skin, and one offal piece), providing approximately 300 kcal, 22g protein, 16g fat, and 8g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for roasted chicken thigh with skin (NDB 05165), chicken skin (NDB 05107), and chicken liver cooked (NDB 05030), blended in approximate proportions reflecting a mixed 5-skewer yakitori order (thigh ~55%, skin ~25%, liver ~20%). Sodium is elevated to reflect tare glaze contribution (~397mg/100g) consistent with published Japanese restaurant sodium data. Input record values cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy and found consistent; values retained. Japan MEXT database was listed in the input but is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved equivalent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yakitori with tare sauce. GI ~30 is estimated by analogy with soy-sauce-glazed grilled chicken and sweet soy-based marinades documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The carbohydrate contribution (~5.4g/100g, ~8g per 150g serving) derives almost entirely from the tare glaze (mirin and sugar); the chicken itself contributes negligible carbohydrate. GL per 150g serving = (30 × 8) / 100 = 2.4. This is a low-GI, very-low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tare glaze (~397mg/100g; ~596mg per 150g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should be aware that a standard 5-skewer tare yakitori serving contributes approximately 596mg sodium (~25% of the 2,300mg daily limit); choosing shio (salt-seasoned) yakitori instead reduces sodium by approximately 400mg per serving and is the preferred option for blood-pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (GL ~2.4 per 150g serving) with high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yakitori with tare sauce has a GI of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of only 2.4 per standard 5-skewer serving, making it one of the lowest-GL Japanese restaurant options; the dominant macronutrients are protein (22g) and fat (16g) per serving, producing minimal postprandial glycaemic excursion and making it suitable as a T2DM-friendly meal component when paired with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.2g/100g; ~4.8g per serving) and moderate sodium from tare glaze
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 150g yakitori serving provides approximately 4.8g saturated fat and 596mg sodium; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit tare-glazed yakitori to occasional consumption, prefer shio seasoning to reduce sodium, and choose thigh-only or breast skewers over skin-heavy orders to reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low postprandial glycaemia, high iron and B12 from liver skewers, minimal HCA production from binchotan charcoal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Binchotan charcoal grilling produces minimal heterocyclic amines compared to direct-flame BBQ; the low-GL profile supports anti-inflammatory dietary goals, and inclusion of chicken liver skewers (kimo) provides approximately 2.4mg iron and 1.5mcg B12 per 30g skewer, supporting micronutrient needs in RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yakitori (焼き鳥) are Japanese grilled chicken skewers, a staple of izakaya (Japanese gastropub) dining. A standard order consists of 5 skewers (~150g edible weight) featuring a mixed composition of chicken thigh, skin, and one offal piece (commonly liver or cartilage), grilled over binchotan white charcoal and brushed with tare — a sweet-salty glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The result is tender, slightly charred chicken with a caramelised, umami-rich glaze. Per 150g serving: approximately 300 kcal, 22g protein, 16g fat (4.8g saturated), 8g carbohydrate, GI ~30, GL ~2.4, and ~596mg sodium (tare version) or ~200mg sodium (shio/salt version). Yakitori is one of the lowest-GL, most protein-efficient Japanese restaurant options and is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management. Chicken liver skewers (kimo) are exceptionally micronutrient-dense, providing ~2.4mg iron and ~1.5mcg B12 per 30g skewer. For hypertensive patients, requesting shio (salt) seasoning instead of tare reduces sodium by approximately 400mg per 5-skewer serving. Available at Japanese izakaya restaurants across Singapore including Torikizoku, Birdman, and Ishinabe.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for roasted chicken thigh with skin, chicken skin, and cooked chicken liver in blended proportions. The Japan MEXT database cited in the input is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2; USDA SR Legacy is used as the approved equivalent. Values are internally consistent and plausible for mixed yakitori. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, not a direct measurement. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture data for this composite mixed yakitori preparation (thigh, skin, and liver blend with tare glaze applied) is not directly reported as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; the blended preparation method and tare glaze absorption make a reliable water_g figure unavailable from any approved source without direct laboratory measurement. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.1g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.3g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for roasted chicken thigh with skin and are approximate. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for grilled chicken per USDA SR Legacy). The input source (Japan MEXT Standard Tables of Food Composition, 8th Edition) is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list; nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and found consistent; USDA FoodData Central is cited as the approved source. Sodium value of 397mg/100g reflects tare-glazed preparation; shio (salt-only) yakitori would have approximately 130–150mg/100g sodium. GI ~30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tare yakitori exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Two is_default:true portions were present in the input; the 150g (5-skewer) serving is used as the primary serving size for GL calculation as it represents the standard izakaya order.
